name: String of Pearls
description: String of Pearls (Senecio rowleyanus and related Senecio spp.) is a distinctive succulent houseplant with bead-like leaves that is moderately toxic to cats. It contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs) — the same liver-damaging compounds found in Ragwort — along with other irritant compounds. All parts of the plant are toxic.
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: pyrrolizidine alkaloids and irritant compounds throughout the plant; the round bead-like leaves may attract cats
Mechanism: PAs are metabolised to reactive pyrroles in the liver, causing hepatocellular necrosis; cumulative damage can lead to liver failure
Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, lethargy; chronic exposure may cause weight loss and jaundice as liver damage accumulates
The dangling bead-like appearance is particularly appealing to cats — relocate to spaces cats cannot access
Seek veterinary care if significant ingestion occurs

symptoms.name: Vomiting and diarrhea
symptoms.notes: If plant pieces are found in the cat's mouth, they should be removed and the mouth should be rinsed with water. Veterinary attention is recommended, especially if a large amount was eaten1. 2. Drooling

symptoms.name: Drooling
symptoms.notes: Drooling typically occurs as a direct response to oral irritation from chewing the plant parts or sap1. 3. Lethargy

symptoms.name: Lethargy
symptoms.notes: Lethargy indicates a systemic reaction to the plant's toxins. If a cat becomes noticeably lethargic after eating the plant, the sources strongly advise contacting a veterinarian1. 4. Skin and Eye Irritation

symptoms.name: Irritation of the skin and eyes
symptoms.notes: This localized irritation is specifically caused by direct physical contact with the plant's sap. Rinsing the cat's paws (and mouth) with water is recommended to remove the irritant sap
